title: Bonuses for Cost-Cutters Act of 2025
stage_label: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 40 - 0.
plain_summary: This law gives cash bonuses to federal workers who find ways the government is wasting money. Workers can get up to twenty thousand dollars if they spot spending that is not needed. When workers find wasteful spending, their agency must ask the President to cut that money from the budget. The law makes the bonus amounts twice as big as they used to be.
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 40 - 0.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Bonuses for Cost-Cutters Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Cost Savings Enhancements
sections.text: This section makes changes to existing federal law about giving cash awards to government workers who save money. First, it adds a new definition for wasteful expenses. Wasteful expenses are money set aside for worker salaries, daily operations, or similar purposes that an employee identifies as wasteful and that the agency's Chief Financial Officer decides is not actually needed. The law increases the maximum cash award amount from ten thousand dollars to twenty thousand dollars. It gives agency heads the power to pay cash awards to employees who identify wasteful expenses that result in cost savings. When the Chief Financial Officer determines that wasteful expenses identified by an employee meet the requirements, the agency head must notify the President so the expenses can be proposed for cancellation under federal budget rules. For agencies without a Chief Financial Officer, the agency head must choose an employee to make these decisions about wasteful expenses. Each agency must provide yearly information about these waste disclosures, including descriptions of each disclosure that had merit and the number and amount of cash awards given. Workers in Inspector General offices and certain other ineligible employees cannot receive these cash awards. The Office of Personnel Management must make sure each agency follows these rules and report to Congress yearly about whether agencies are following them. The Comptroller General must report to Congress about how well this program is working three years after this law starts and then every three years for six more years, including any suggestions for changes.
